topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I smoothed the lapel of my blazer for the tenth time, the crisp fabric feeling suddenly too tight across my chest. Across the desk, Liam was laughing, a bright, easy sound that grated on my nerves. He was recounting an anecdote about his previous interview, his gestures expansive, his eyes sparkling with unearned confidence. I watched the way he leaned forward, the slight tilt of his head as he spoke, cataloging every micro-expression. My jaw tightened, a small, involuntary clench that I quickly masked by taking a slow, deliberate sip of cold coffee. This competition felt less like a shared opportunity and more like a carefully laid trap, and I was acutely aware of every move I made, every glance I dared to cast toward him.

[story 2]

"So, you’re really leaning into the creative aspect of the proposal," Liam said, his voice warm and entirely too casual as he leaned back in his chair. I nodded, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face. Internally, I was running a rapid-fire assessment of his recent accomplishments, cross-referencing them against my own portfolio in a silent, frantic calculation. Every time he spoke about his experience, I focused intently on the details—the specific software he mentioned, the projects he highlighted—trying to find the slightest imperfection, the slippage in his narrative. My hands, resting on the edge of the mahogany table, were cool and steady, betraying none of the internal tension coiling in my stomach. I needed to be absolutely certain of the ground beneath my feet before I made my next move.

[story 3]

I stood by the window, pretending to study the cityscape, but my attention was entirely fixed on the door. Liam was pacing near the water cooler, his energy radiating outward, a palpable force that made the air around him feel charged. He was talking to Sarah, his voice pitched just loud enough to carry, full of practiced charm. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my sternum, a need to maintain absolute composure, to keep my expression neutral and unreadable. I kept my breathing even, a slow, measured rhythm that I was certain he couldn't detect. Each time he turned his attention toward me, I met his gaze for just a fraction of a second too long, a brief, sharp assessment passing between us, before I looked away, the subtle hardening around my eyes the only evidence of the internal scrutiny I was subjecting myself to.
